entry.narrative_markdown:
# Proof Summary: Was Thomas Flynn of Ballingarry, County Tipperary, the Father of Patrick Flynn (b. ~1845, d. 1908)?

**Research question:** Was Thomas Flynn of Ballingarry, County Tipperary, the father of Patrick Flynn, born about 1845 in Ireland and died 12 March 1908 in Schuylkill County, Pennsylvania?

**Conclusion:** The primary evidence disproves the claim. The Thomas Flynn of Ballingarry named in online trees as Patrick's father was buried in November 1842 — nearly three years before Patrick was born. A man who died in 1842 cannot have fathered a child born about 1845. The claim is not merely unsupported; it is chronologically impossible.

---

## Identity of the Candidate Father

An unsourced FamilySearch Family Tree entry names Thomas Flynn of Ballingarry, County Tipperary (born about 1800, wife Bridget), as the father of Patrick Flynn. Several derivative online trees repeat this assertion, all tracing to the same unsourced entry.

The Ballingarry (County Tipperary) Catholic parish burial register records the interment of Thomas Flynn, of Ballingarry, on 14 November 1842.¹ The entry notes his approximate age (~42), his townland of Ballingarry, and identifies his wife as Bridget — identifiers that match precisely those given in the online tree for the candidate father. This entry is an original source; the information was recorded by the officiating priest at or near the time of burial (primary information, direct evidence of Thomas's death). It establishes without ambiguity that the Thomas Flynn of Ballingarry described in the online tree died in November 1842.

---

## Patrick Flynn's Birth Date

Patrick Flynn appears in the 1850 U.S. Federal Census for Schuylkill County, Pennsylvania, as a child aged 5, born in Ireland, residing in the household of Bridget Flynn, widow.² Age 5 at the time of the 1850 enumeration implies a birth of about 1845. The informant for the census entry is unknown — an unknown household member or neighbor reporting to the enumerator — yielding indeterminate information quality; the age-derived birth year is indirect evidence. This estimate is independently corroborated by Patrick's 1908 Pennsylvania death certificate, which records his age at death as approximately 63, implying a birth of about 1845; the informant on that certificate was James Brown, Patrick's son-in-law, whose report constitutes secondary information (family member not present at the birth), yielding indirect evidence of birth year.³ The convergence of two independent original records — one from the beginning and one from the end of Patrick's life — strongly establishes a birth of about 1845.

---

## The Chronological Impossibility

Thomas Flynn of Ballingarry was buried 14 November 1842. Patrick Flynn was born approximately 1845 — a gap of roughly two and three-quarter to three years. No plausible adjustment to Patrick's estimated birth year closes this gap. Even allowing for census age error of ±1 to 2 years, a birth as early as 1843 would still postdate Thomas's burial by at least one year, and there is no evidence supporting a birth that early. The death of Thomas Flynn in 1842 is established by a contemporaneous original record; Patrick's birth about 1845 is corroborated by two independent sources from different decades. The chronological incompatibility is decisive.

---

## Origin of the False Claim

The parentage claim originates in an unsourced FamilySearch Family Tree entry and was propagated by derivative online trees that cite no primary record. No source supporting the Thomas Flynn–Patrick Flynn parentage was located despite targeted searches of the relevant records. The likely mechanism was a name-and-place coincidence: "Thomas Flynn of Ballingarry" connected to "Patrick Flynn of Irish origin in Pennsylvania" without date verification. This type of unverified copying is a known hazard of collaborative online trees and does not constitute genealogical evidence.

---

## Conclusion

The claim that Thomas Flynn of Ballingarry, County Tipperary, was the father of Patrick Flynn (b. ~1845, d. 1908 Schuylkill County, Pennsylvania) is disproved. An original record — the Ballingarry Catholic parish burial register — establishes Thomas's death on 14 November 1842, before Patrick's birth about 1845 as corroborated by the 1850 census and the 1908 death certificate. The claim is chronologically impossible and originates from an unsourced online tree entry. Research to identify Patrick Flynn's actual father remains open.

*This conclusion represents the best determination supported by current evidence. Should primary records emerge that alter the established dates or identities, reassessment would be warranted.*

---

**Citations**

¹ Ballingarry (County Tipperary, Ireland), Catholic parish registers, burials, Thomas Flynn, 14 November 1842; National Library of Ireland, Catholic Parish Registers; digital image, FamilySearch (https://www.familysearch.org/ark:/61903/1:1:BALL1842), accessed 4 May 2026.

² 1850 U.S. Census, Schuylkill County, Pennsylvania, population schedule, dwelling 142, family 150, Bridget Flynn household; NARA microfilm M432, roll 810; digital image, FamilySearch (https://www.familysearch.org/ark:/61903/1:1:M1850W), accessed 2 May 2026.

³ Pennsylvania Department of Health, death certificate no. 4521 (1908), Patrick Flynn; Pennsylvania State Archives, Harrisburg; digital image, FamilySearch (https://www.familysearch.org/ark:/61903/1:1:MDEF), accessed 6 May 2026.

Patrick's birth year ~1845 is correctly derived and corroborated by a_002 (1850 census, age 5) and a_006 (1908 death cert, age ~63). The chronological impossibility conclusion is factually sound: a man dead in 1842 cannot father a child born ~1845. All claims are sourced and substantiated. No fabrications or unsupported assertions present.

dimensions.name: Completeness
dimensions.rationale: The skill addressed all required elements: (1) correctly determined the conclusion should proceed despite unlinked fact assertions a_002/a_006, applying the precondition gate correctly (persons I1 and I2 are identity-linked); (2) wrote the disproved tier conclusion in ps_001; (3) did not update tree.gedcomx.json (correct for tier below probable); (4) stamped project.updated; (5) produced a self-contained narrative with proper citations, evidence discussion, and conflict analysis (none present). All assertions a_001 through a_006 are addressed. No required step omitted.

dimensions.name: Tool Arguments
dimensions.rationale: Both MCP tool calls matched live fixtures with ok: true responses. First call (research_append, proof_summaries, append) correctly passed projectPath, section, op, and entry with all required fields (question_id: q_001, tier: disproved, vehicle: summary, supporting_assertion_ids all six assertions, narrative_markdown, exhaustive_search_summary). Second call (research_append, project, update) correctly passed empty fields dict to stamp updated timestamp. No fixture_not_found errors; all args semantically correct.

dimensions.name: Tier justification
dimensions.rationale: The disproved tier is justified by affirmative refutation via chronological impossibility. The narrative explicitly explains why disproved (not merely unsupported): 'the claim is chronologically impossible, not merely unsupported.' The evidence (Thomas buried 1842, Patrick born ~1845) directly contradicts the parentage claim. The skill correctly contrasts this with lower tiers by noting the chronological gap is 'decisive' and 'no plausible adjustment' closes it. Tier is appropriate and reasoning is explicit.

dimensions.name: Narrative standalone
dimensions.rationale: The narrative is fully self-contained. Every assertion is described in prose: burial register entry with date/age/identifiers (§Identity of Candidate Father); census age-to-birth conversion and death cert corroboration (§Patrick Flynn's Birth Date); chronological gap analysis (§Chronological Impossibility); origin discussion (§Origin of False Claim). Citations are inline with superscript references to full citations at end. Conflict resolution is addressed (none found, explicitly noted). Tier is declared. A reader with no access to research.json can follow completely.

dimensions.name: Evidence completeness
dimensions.rationale: All six assertions are cited in the narrative: a_001 (Patrick identity link), a_002 (1850 census age), a_003 (Thomas burial, decisive fact), a_004 (Thomas identity, burial register), a_005 (Patrick identity link), a_006 (1908 death cert age). All resolved conflicts are addressed (conflicts: [], none present). The narrative does not selectively present only supporting evidence; it discusses the unlinked fact assertions a_002/a_006 as advisory (corroborating birth year) and incorporates them into the reasoning. No inconvenient evidence is omitted.

dimensions.name: Proof-conclusion fit
dimensions.rationale: The chosen form—Summary—correctly matches the evidence structure. A disproved conclusion on straightforward chronological grounds with no competing candidates or contradictions is appropriately a Summary, not an Argument. The narrative follows Summary form: states the question and conclusion upfront, discusses identity/corroboration/incompatibility, addresses origin, and concludes. Single-track refutation by primary record (burial register) is not complex enough to require Argument machinery. Form and structure align.

dimensions.name: Tree encoding
dimensions.rationale: The concluded tier is disproved (below probable threshold). Per the rubric and test context, no tree relationship write is expected; the tree must remain unchanged. The skill correctly did not write a ParentChild link and tree.gedcomx.json remains byte-for-byte identical. This is the correct behavior for a tier below probable. N/A applies; tree encoding is not graded for disproved conclusions.

output.text_response:
Now I have everything I need. Let me assess ps_001 thoroughly against all five GPS components.I now have a complete picture of ps_001 and all surrounding evidence. Here is the full GPS assessment — this is review mode, so nothing is written to research.json or the tree.

---

## GPS Assessment of ps_001 — Parentage of Patrick Flynn

**Proof summary:** `ps_001` | Tier: `probable` | Form: `summary`
**Supporting assertions cited:** a_004 (1850 census relationship), a_010 (1860 census relationship), a_013 (death cert — father named)
**Conflict cited:** c_001 (birthplace: Ireland vs. Pennsylvania) — resolved

---

### GPS Component 1 — Reasonably Exhaustive Research

**Verdict: Not fully met — correctly acknowledged; consistent with `probable` tier.**

The exhaustive declaration for q_001 is `declared: false`. Research conducted through ps_001 includes:
- 1850 census: FamilySearch (original image), Ancestry (derivative index), MyHeritage (nil return)
- 1860 census: FamilySearch (original image)
- 1908 death certificate: FamilySearch (original)

Not yet searched (as of this proof): 1870, 1880, and 1900 censuses; Thomas Flynn probate (pli_006 is in_progress). The narrative explicitly states these gaps: *"the 1870, 1880, and 1900 censuses have not been searched, and Thomas Flynn's probate records have not been located."* That transparency is exactly what the GPS requires when a conclusion is written before research is exhaustive. The `probable` tier is the correct response — the proof does not overclaim.

**No defect here.** Exhaustive research is a precondition for `proved`, not for `probable`.

---

### GPS Component 2 — Complete, Accurate Citations

**Verdict: Met for all cited sources.**

Three citations appear in the narrative. Evaluating each:

| Source | Citation | Assessment |
|---|---|---|
| **src_001** — 1850 Census | NARA M432/roll 810; FamilySearch ARK; dwelling/family numbers; access date | Complete and accurate ✓ |
| **src_003** — 1860 Census | NARA M653/roll 1141; FamilySearch ARK; dwelling/family numbers; access date | Complete and accurate ✓ |
| **src_004** — Death Certificate | Certificate no. 4521; Pennsylvania State Archives; FamilySearch; access date | Complete and accurate ✓ |

The Ancestry derivative (src_002) is not cited — a defensible editorial choice, since it indexes the same underlying original and the proof correctly relies on the original. The narrative cites only records whose evidence it discusses, which is sound practice.

---

### GPS Component 3 — Analysis and Correlation of Evidence

**Verdict: Met.**

The three-layer classification is applied correctly to every cited source in the narrative:

- **1850 census** — original / indeterminate / indirect. The narrative correctly notes that the 1850 census does not state relationships and explains the inferred nature of the parentage conclusion (household position, shared surname). Informant identified as likely a household member; uncertainty acknowledged.
- **1860 census** — original / indeterminate / indirect. The narrative again correctly notes that the relationship column was not introduced until 1880, so this too is inferred. Consistent with a_010's classification.
- **Death certificate** — original / secondary / direct. The narrative identifies James Brown as son-in-law, explains why he is a secondary informant for parentage (not a witness to Patrick's birth, reporting what he was told), and correctly distinguishes this from the death facts (where the attending physician is primary).

Correlation is coherent and clearly presented: two independent decennial enumerations in the same household, then a direct naming at death, converge on Thomas Flynn as father. The narrative does not overstate what the census evidence shows. The assessment section correctly characterizes the evidentiary weight of each source type.

No classification gaps affecting the cited assertions (a_004, a_010, a_013 all carry reasoned three-layer values with informant analysis).

---

### GPS Component 4 — Resolution of Conflicting Evidence

**Verdict: Met.**

One conflict is registered and cited (c_001): Patrick's birthplace as Ireland (1850 and 1860 censuses) vs. Pennsylvania (1908 death certificate). The resolution walks through all four required steps:

1. **Acknowledges the conflict explicitly** — yes, stated in the "Conflict Resolution" section
2. **Analyzes reliability of each side** — yes: contemporary household-informant sources vs. a 63-year-later recollection by a secondary informant (son-in-law)
3. **States which is more credible and why** — yes: Ireland accepted, with GPS preponderance reasoning (contemporary + closer informant outweighs later + secondary)
4. **Explains why the error exists** — yes: son-in-law may have confused Patrick's Pennsylvania residence with birthplace, or may simply not have known Patrick was born in Ireland

All four steps are present. The conflict is collateral to the parentage conclusion (birthplace, not parentage), so it does not cap the tier. No other conflicts appear in the data.

---

### GPS Component 5 — Soundly Reasoned, Coherently Written Conclusion

**Verdict: Met.**

Several specific checks:

- **Tier and phrasing aligned.** The tier is `probable`; the narrative opens "Patrick Flynn is **Probably** the son of Thomas Flynn." No tier/phrasing mismatch.
- **Form is appropriate.** A `summary` is correct here: multiple sources must be correlated, but there are no significant unresolved conflicts, no competing candidates, and no indirect-only evidence chain requiring a full argument. Selection test passes.
- **Logical organization.** Conclusion stated upfront → evidence numbered by source (not research chronology) → conflict resolution → limitations acknowledged → citations. Reader can independently evaluate the chain.
- **Self-contained.** Readable without research.json; uploadable as a FamilySearch document.
- **Limitations transparent.** The assessment section clearly states what is missing and what would advance the tier.
- **Research scope demonstrated.** Repositories named; nil result (MyHeritage) noted in the exhaustive search summary.

One minor observation, not a defect: the narrative labels all three sources as "Original Source" in parentheses immediately after naming them. The death certificate is indeed original as a source; however, the parentage information it contains is secondary (James Brown's report). The narrative *does* explain this correctly in the body text — the parentage label in the header is just shorthand for source type, and the informant analysis follows — so a reader is not misled. This is fine as written.

---

### Overall Verdict

**ps_001 is a GPS-conformant `probable` conclusion.** Components 2–5 are fully met as the proof stands. Component 1 is intentionally incomplete (the correct posture for an in-progress project at the `probable` tier), and the narrative is transparent about every gap. The tier is well-calibrated, the form is appropriate, and the conflict is properly resolved. There is nothing to correct.

---

### Evidence Gaps for Future Rounds

The following sources exist in research.json but have **no extracted assertions** and are not cited in ps_001. These are not flaws in the current proof — they are the agenda for the next round.

| Source | Record | Potential Impact | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| **src_005** | St. Mary's Catholic Church, baptism of Patrick Flynn, 1845 | **HIGH — could shift tier to `proved`** | An original church record naming Thomas Flynn as father would be primary/direct evidence. The tree already encodes two siblings (Mary 1841, James 1847) as children of Thomas from this same register (tree S5, relationships R2/R3) — if Patrick's baptism names the same father, that convergence across four independent records is compelling. Citation is rough (missing volume/page locator). |
| **src_006** | Thomas Flynn will, Schuylkill County, 1881 | **HIGH — could shift tier to `proved`** | A will naming Patrick as son is direct evidence from Thomas himself — original source, primary information. This is pli_006, currently in_progress. Citation is rough (missing Will Book volume and page). |
| **src_008** | Deed, Thomas Flynn to Patrick Flynn, 1881 | **MODERATE — corroborating** | A property transfer from Thomas to Patrick implies a relationship, but rarely proves parentage alone. Adds to the convergent pattern as indirect evidence. Citation is rough (missing Deed Book/page, execution/recording dates). |
| **src_007** | PA birth cert, Mary Elizabeth Flynn, 1905 | **LOWER — incidental** | A grandchild's birth cert might name Patrick's parentage or further situate the family. Likely secondary-to-tertiary for the parentage question. Citation is rough (wrong `who` field; missing certificate number, birth date, place). |
| **src_009** | Patrick Flynn obituary, *Pottsville Republican*, 1908 | **LOWER — corroborating** | Authored source; lowest evidential weight. May name surviving relatives or biographical details consistent with parentage. Citation is rough (missing article title, exact date, page, column). |

**Would any of these change the tier?** If src_005 (baptism) yields a Patrick Flynn entry naming Thomas Flynn as father, *and* src_006 (will) names Patrick as Thomas's son, the combination of two independent original/primary/direct records plus the two census co-enumerations and death certificate would almost certainly support advancement to **`proved`** — provided research is then declared exhaustive and the phrasing is updated to match. Either one alone, combined with what exists, would reinforce `probable` and build toward `proved`. The deed, obituary, and descendant birth cert add corroboration but are unlikely to be decisive on their own.

**Also from the tree:** Relationships R2 (Thomas → Mary Flynn) and R3 (Thomas → James Flynn) are already encoded using the baptismal register — siblings of Patrick. Their presence in the tree but absence from research.json assertions means this evidence path hasn't been run through the extraction workflow yet. When src_005 assertions are extracted, the sibling pattern strengthens the household identification considerably.

---

**Recommended next step:** Extract assertions from src_006 (Thomas Flynn will — pli_006 is in_progress) and src_005 (baptismal record) via `record-extraction`, then re-run `research-exhaustiveness` for q_001. If the will names Patrick as son, revisit ps_001 for a tier upgrade.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Narrative standalone
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: The narrative is fully self-contained and publication-ready. aggregated_dimensions.name: Evidence completeness
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: All 12 assertions are cited and incorporated into the narrative without selective omission. aggregated_dimensions.rationale: The Proof Argument form is the correct choice for this evidence shape. The narrative is structured as an argument precisely because it requires the reader to follow a chain of reasoning across two competing candidates with only indirect evidence and failed elimination. No persons were linked; the tree remains unchanged as intended for conclusions below the `probable` threshold.

The evidence in full:
- **src_001** (1850 Census, Thomas Flynn household): Patrick Flynn, age 5 — child position *inferred*, not stated; informant unknown. Indeterminate / indirect.
- **src_002** (1850 Census, Michael Flynn household): Patrick Flynn, age 6 — same limitations. Indeterminate / indirect.
- **src_003** (1860 Census): Adult Patrick, age 15, *boarding* with no Flynn present — negative-bridging evidence; original / primary / indirect.

The evidence does not even preponderate toward one candidate — this is below *possible* for any named answer to the question.

entry.narrative_markdown:
# Who Was the Father of Patrick Flynn (b. ~1845, d. 1908, Schuylkill County, Pennsylvania)?

**Proof Argument — Tier: Not Proved**

## Research Question

Who was the father of Patrick Flynn, born circa 1845 in Ireland and died 12 March 1908 in Schuylkill County, Pennsylvania?

## Sources and Evidence

### The 1850 Census — Two Candidate Households

The 1850 federal census for Schuylkill County is the only census year that could capture Patrick as a child within a parental household. It reveals two Patrick Flynns of broadly matching age, both born in Ireland, enumerated in the same county.

**Candidate Household A** places a Patrick Flynn, age 5, in the household of Thomas Flynn (dwelling 84, family 91).¹ The 1850 census did not record family relationships; the position of this child in the household is *consistent with* a parent–child relationship to Thomas Flynn, but that relationship is inferred from household structure, not stated. The informant is unknown — most likely a household member or a neighbor reporting to the enumerator. This assertion is classified as an original source carrying indeterminate information (the informant's identity and proximity are unknown) and indirect evidence on the question of paternity (the source does not name Thomas as the father; that inference depends on the unverified assumption that this child is our subject).

**Candidate Household B** places a Patrick Flynn, age 6, in the household of Michael Flynn (dwelling 210, family 233), a different enumeration district.² The same analytical limitations apply: the relationship to Michael Flynn is inferred from household position, not stated; the informant is unknown. Classified identically — original source, indeterminate information, indirect evidence.

The one-year age difference between candidates A (age 5, implying birth ~1845) and B (age 6, implying birth ~1844) falls well within the normal margin of census age-reporting error for a subject born approximately 1845. Neither age can be used to prefer one household. No other feature — occupation, neighborhood, naming pattern, or geographic proximity — distinguishes the two candidates or associates adult Patrick definitively with one rather than the other.

### The 1860 Census — Negative-Bridging Evidence

Patrick Flynn, age 15, born in Ireland, working as a coal laborer, was located as a boarder in the household of Owen Donnelly, Schuylkill County, in the 1860 federal census.³ This source is original; the informant was a witness to Patrick's actual residence (primary information); the evidence bearing on parentage is indirect — it does not name a father, but it shows where Patrick was living at age 15.

Neither Thomas Flynn nor Michael Flynn appears in the Donnelly household or as a near neighbor in the same enumeration district. This is *negative-bridging evidence*: by 1860, Patrick was living independently of any Flynn household, and the 1860 record provides no chain linking him back to either 1850 candidate. The absence cannot be attributed to parental death with any confidence, because neither Thomas nor Michael Flynn has been traced forward from 1850.

### The 1908 Death Certificate — Father Reported Unknown

Patrick Flynn died 12 March 1908 in Schuylkill County. His death certificate (no. 4521) is an original record.⁴ The attending physician — a witness to the death — supplied the cause and date of death: that portion is primary information and direct evidence of Patrick's death. The informant for the family-history fields was James Brown, Patrick's son-in-law. Brown reported Patrick's father's name as "unknown."

A son-in-law, not present at Patrick's birth and one generation removed, is a secondary informant with limited knowledge of Patrick's parentage. His report constitutes *negative evidence* on the question: the one document that formally asks for the father's name leaves it blank. This is not a transcription gap or an enumerator's oversight — the informant affirmatively stated ignorance.

### Catholic Parish Registers — No Baptism Located

A search of FamilySearch and National Library of Ireland Catholic Parish Registers for a Patrick Flynn baptism (Schuylkill County and the County Tipperary emigrant cohort, 1843–1850) located no matching record. A Catholic baptism would have named the father directly in a contemporary, original document — it is the one record type that would close the question. Its absence leaves the direct-evidence gap unfilled. This is a nil-result, not evidence of non-baptism; unindexed or undigitized registers may survive.

## Analysis and Correlation

All four targeted record types were pursued; together they point to the same conclusion: the identity of Patrick Flynn's father cannot be established from surviving records.

The two 1850 candidate households present mutually exclusive possibilities of equal evidentiary weight. Preferring Thomas Flynn over Michael Flynn — or vice versa — would require distinguishing evidence that does not exist in any of the searched records. Each hypothesis rests on a single unverified assumption: that the child in that household is our subject. Because neither assumption can be confirmed or contradicted, neither hypothesis can be elevated above the other.

The 1860 census and 1908 death certificate, the adult records that most reliably establish Patrick's identity, contribute nothing to the parentage question except negative evidence. The 1860 record shows Patrick had already separated from any Flynn household before age 15. The 1908 certificate records the father as unknown — a finding that, while secondary, reflects the likely reality that Patrick himself did not reliably transmit this information to his family.

The Catholic register search eliminated the one pathway to direct, contemporary evidence of paternity.

No conflicts exist among these records.

## Conclusion

The question of Patrick Flynn's father is **not proved**. Research has been declared reasonably exhaustive. Complete, accurate citations have been supplied for all sources examined. Analysis and classification have been performed on each assertion. No conflicting evidence requires resolution. Applying the fifth GPS component — a soundly reasoned conclusion — leads to the finding that the evidence is insufficient to name Patrick's father.

Two hypotheses remain open and equally unresolved: (h_001) that Patrick's father was Thomas Flynn of dwelling 84, and (h_002) that his father was Michael Flynn of dwelling 210. Neither hypothesis is supported by evidence that rises to any definitive tier independently, because identifying the correct childhood household is the very question that the evidence cannot answer.

Discovery of an unindexed Catholic baptismal record naming Patrick's father, a contemporary family Bible record, an emigration document, or naturalization papers identifying the father would change this assessment and should prompt re-evaluation.

---

¹ 1850 U.S. Census, Schuylkill County, Pennsylvania, population schedule, dwelling 84, family 91, Thomas Flynn household; NARA microfilm publication M432, roll 810; digital image, FamilySearch.org, accessed 2 May 2026.

² 1850 U.S. Census, Schuylkill County, Pennsylvania, population schedule, dwelling 210, family 233, Michael Flynn household; NARA microfilm publication M432, roll 810; digital image, FamilySearch.org, accessed 2 May 2026.

³ 1860 U.S. Census, Schuylkill County, Pennsylvania, population schedule, dwelling 305, Owen Donnelly household, Patrick Flynn (boarder); NARA microfilm publication M653, roll 1141; digital image, FamilySearch.org, accessed 3 May 2026.

⁴ Pennsylvania Department of Health, death certificate no. 4521 (1908), Patrick Flynn; Pennsylvania State Archives, Harrisburg; digital image, FamilySearch.org, accessed 5 May 2026.

entry.narrative_markdown:
## Proof Summary: Possible Parentage of Patrick Flynn

**Research Question:** Who were the parents of Patrick Flynn (born about 1845, probably in Ireland; died 1908 in Schuylkill County, Pennsylvania)?

**Conclusion:** Based on available evidence, Thomas Flynn of Schuylkill County, Pennsylvania, is a *possible* father of Patrick Flynn. A single indirect inference from the 1850 U.S. Census supports this hypothesis. No corroborating records have yet been searched, and research has not been declared reasonably exhaustive. This conclusion may be advanced as additional sources are examined.

---

### Evidence

The sole record examined is the **1850 U.S. Federal Census**, population schedule for Schuylkill County, Pennsylvania, dwelling 84, family 91 (original source; NARA microfilm M432, roll 810; digital image, FamilySearch.org, accessed 1 May 2026).¹ This record lists a Patrick Flynn, age 5, born in Ireland, as a member of the household headed by Thomas Flynn. Thomas is recorded as age 32, also born in Ireland.

**Source classification:** The 1850 census is an *original source* — the government's contemporaneous enumeration, not a later transcription or derivative. For the co-residence fact (Patrick's presence in the Thomas Flynn household), the information is *primary*: the enumerator personally visited the dwelling and recorded its occupants as a direct witness. For the relationship between Patrick and Thomas, however, the information is *indeterminate*: the 1850 census does not record relationships between household members. The label "child of Thomas Flynn" is an inference constructed by the researcher from household position and shared surname, not a statement made by any informant. The evidence this record provides for parentage is therefore *indirect* — consistent with a father-son relationship, but not directly establishing one.

---

### Assessment

The presence of a 5-year-old Patrick Flynn in the Thomas Flynn household in Schuylkill County, Pennsylvania, in 1850 is a credible lead consistent with a father-son relationship. Thomas Flynn's approximate age (32 in 1850, born about 1818) is biologically consistent with fathering a child born about 1845. The shared surname, shared place of birth (Ireland), and geographic continuity — Patrick is known to have lived and died in Schuylkill County — lend additional plausibility to the hypothesis.

However, significant limitations prevent a stronger conclusion:

1. **The 1850 census does not state relationships.** A same-surname child in a household could be a son, nephew, grandchild, or an unrelated boarder with the same name. Household co-residence alone cannot establish parentage.
2. **Only one record has been searched.** The 1860 U.S. Census — which would show whether Patrick remained in Thomas Flynn's household at approximately age 15 — and Patrick's 1908 death certificate — which may name his parents directly — have not been consulted. Neither has any church record, immigration record, or probate document.
3. **Research is not exhaustive.** Because critical corroborating sources remain unsearched, a higher confidence tier is not warranted at this time.

---

### Path to a Stronger Conclusion

This conclusion may be advanced to *probable* or *proved* if:
- The 1860 U.S. Census for Schuylkill County shows Patrick Flynn (age approximately 15) still residing in Thomas Flynn's household;
- Patrick Flynn's 1908 Schuylkill County death certificate names Thomas Flynn as his father; or
- Additional independent sources (church baptismal records, immigration records, or probate documents) corroborate the relationship.

---

*This conclusion represents the best answer the current evidence supports and is subject to revision if new evidence emerges.*

¹ 1850 U.S. Census, Schuylkill County, Pennsylvania, population schedule, dwelling 84, family 91, Thomas Flynn household; NARA microfilm publication M432, roll 810; digital image, FamilySearch.org, accessed 1 May 2026.

output.text_response:
## Preconditions Gate

Running the mandatory check before any tier change.

**Assertions linked to q_001:** a_004, a_005, a_008, a_009, a_010, a_012, a_013

**Step 2 — Classification.** Every assertion carries a real, reasoned `information_quality` and `evidence_type` with `informant`/`informant_proximity` analysis. ✅ All pass.

**Step 3 — person_evidence.**
- **I1 (Patrick):** `pe_001` → `a_001` (name, confident). ✅
- **I2 (Thomas):** `pe_003` → `a_005` (name, probable). ✅
- **Advisory (non-blocking):** `a_008`, `a_009`, `a_012` carry no direct `pe_` links, but their persons are already identity-resolved above.

**Step 4 — Conflicts.**
- `c_001` (birthplace: Ireland vs. Pennsylvania): status is **`unresolved`** in the conflicts record (`weighing_analysis: null`, `resolution_rationale: null`, `preferred_assertion_id: null`). The `ps_001` narrative explicitly argues through this conflict (its "Conflict Resolution" section) and lists `c_001` in `resolved_conflict_ids` — so the conflict carries an explicit acknowledgment via the existing proof narrative. Gate passes with a data-consistency note: the conflict record itself was never formally updated to `resolved`.

**Gate: passes** — with that note about the data inconsistency.

---

## Tier Assessment — Why `proved` Is Not Achievable Yet

The GPS `proved` tier requires **all five components** to be fully satisfied. Two block it here:

### 🚫 GPS Component 1 — Reasonably Exhaustive Research (hard block)

`q_001.exhaustive_declaration.declared = false`. The research plan for this question (`pl_002`) explicitly identifies three unsearched record sets:

| Plan item | Status | Record type |
|-----------|--------|-------------|
| pli_004 | completed | 1860 census ✓ |
| pli_005 | completed | Death certificate ✓ |
| **pli_006** | **in_progress** | **Thomas Flynn probate/will** |

Beyond pli_006, the 1870, 1880, and 1900 U.S. censuses for Patrick Flynn in Schuylkill County were never searched. The existing `ps_001` narrative itself states this gap: *"research is not yet exhaustive — the 1870, 1880, and 1900 censuses have not been searched, and Thomas Flynn's probate records have not been located."*

No exhaustive declaration = GPS Component 1 fails = **proved is blocked absolutely**.

### ⚠️ GPS Component 4 — Conflict Resolution (data inconsistency)

`c_001` (birthplace) is narratively resolved in `ps_001` and is a **collateral** fact (not the parentage relationship itself), so it only blocks `proved`, not `probable`. But the conflict record still reads `status: "unresolved"` with all analysis fields null — a data inconsistency that should be cleaned up regardless of tier.

---

## Current Tier: `probable` — Correct and Unchanged

The existing `ps_001` is accurately written at `probable`. No update is warranted. The tree already reflects the relationship: `R1` (ParentChild: I2 Thomas Flynn → I1 Patrick Flynn) with quality-2 source references on all three sources.

---

## What Would Advance to `proved`

Three steps, in order:

1. **Complete the open plan items** — run `search-records` for the 1870, 1880, and 1900 U.S. censuses (Schuylkill County) and finish the Thomas Flynn probate search (pli_006). A will naming Patrick as a son would be strong corroboration.
2. **Declare research exhaustive** — once those searches are done, run `research-exhaustiveness` to evaluate and write the `exhaustive_declaration` on q_001.
3. **Formally resolve c_001** — run `conflict-resolution` to update the conflict record (add `weighing_analysis`, `resolution_rationale`, `preferred_assertion_id`, set `status: "resolved"`). This also clears the data inconsistency.

After all three, re-invoke `proof-conclusion` and the `proved` upgrade will be supportable — provided the new records don't introduce fresh conflicts.

entry.narrative_markdown:
## Proof Summary: The Father of Patrick Flynn (b. ~1845, Ireland; d. 12 March 1908, Schuylkill County, Pennsylvania)

**Research Question:** Who was the father of Patrick Flynn, born about 1845 in Ireland and died 12 March 1908 in Schuylkill County, Pennsylvania?

**Conclusion:** The evidence proves that Thomas Flynn was the father of Patrick Flynn.

---

### Primary-Information Sources

Two independent original sources record the parent-child relationship with primary information, each explicitly naming Thomas Flynn as Patrick's father.

**1845 Irish Catholic Baptismal Register.** The baptismal register of St. Mary's Roman Catholic Church, County Tipperary, Ireland, records the baptism of Patricius Flynn on 12 March 1845, naming the parents as Thomas Flynn and Mary Brennan.[1] This is an *original source*: the register is an autograph entry made by the officiating priest, Rev. Michael O'Connor, a direct witness to the ceremony. The information is *primary*: the parents were present and named themselves to the priest, who had no incentive to misrecord. The evidence is *direct*: the entry explicitly states Thomas Flynn as the father of the child. This contemporaneous entry is the earliest and most reliable evidence of the parent-child relationship.

**Flynn Family Bible.** The birth/family record pages of the Flynn family Bible (Holy Bible, Dublin imprint 1840), held by a descendant, record "Patrick Flynn, b. 1845, son of Thomas Flynn and Mary Brennan."[2] The Bible's imprint (1840) predates all birth entries; a single consistent hand throughout the birth pages supports contemporaneous recording rather than later transcription. The source is *original*. The information is *primary*: the recorder is Thomas Flynn himself — the father — recording his own children's births at the time of the events. The evidence is *direct*. Critically, this record is fully independent of the 1845 baptismal register: a different recorder, a different record type, and a different custodian. It constitutes a second, stand-alone primary-information source on the father-child relationship.

---

### Corroborating Census Evidence

**1850 U.S. Census.** The 1850 federal census for Schuylkill County, Pennsylvania, enumerates Patrick Flynn, age 5 (born Ireland), in dwelling 84 in the household headed by Thomas Flynn (age approximately 32, born Ireland).[3] An Ancestry index of the same census confirms the same household.[4] The source is *original* (NARA microfilm M432, roll 810). Because the 1850 census does not record relationships, the parent-child relationship is *inferred* from household position, shared surname, and consistent ages — making the evidence *indirect*. The enumerator physically visited the dwelling, rendering the residence itself *primary* information, but the identities of the informant for names, ages, and birthplaces are unknown (*indeterminate*).

**1860 U.S. Census.** Patrick Flynn, age 15, again appears in the household of Thomas Flynn in Schuylkill County (dwelling 112, family 119).[5] The same analytical framework applies: *original* source, *indeterminate* information (household informant unknown), *indirect* evidence of the parent-child relationship. The consistent co-residence across two independent enumerations, a decade apart, reinforces the relationship and establishes continuity of the family unit.

---

### Death Certificate Evidence

**1908 Pennsylvania Death Certificate.** The death certificate for Patrick Flynn (certificate no. 4521, died 12 March 1908, Schuylkill County) names Thomas Flynn as the father.[6] The source is *original*. The information on parentage is *secondary*: the informant, James Brown (son-in-law), was not present at Patrick's birth and reported what he understood about his father-in-law's parentage decades after the fact. The evidence is *direct*: the certificate explicitly names Thomas Flynn. As secondary information from a non-witness, this source carries less weight than the contemporaneous baptism and family Bible, but it independently corroborates the parentage conclusion.

---

### Resolution of Conflicting Evidence

One conflict exists: the 1908 death certificate records Patrick's birthplace as Pennsylvania, while the 1850 census, the 1860 census, and the 1845 baptismal register all give Ireland (the baptism confirms County Tipperary specifically).[c_001] This conflict is resolved in favor of Ireland. The two census records are original sources whose informants — most likely household members — were reporting near-contemporary information. The 1845 baptism is primary evidence recorded at the event. The death certificate's birthplace was supplied by James Brown (son-in-law), who was not present at Patrick's birth and was reporting sixty-three years after the fact; he may have confused Patrick's long-time place of residence with his place of birth, or may simply not have known Patrick was born in Ireland before immigrating as a young child. This conflict pertains to birthplace only and has no bearing on the parentage conclusion.

---

### Negative Evidence

A search of the FamilySearch probate index for Schuylkill County (1870–1890) returned no will or estate records for Thomas Flynn.[8] This absence does not contradict the parentage conclusion; Thomas may have died intestate, died outside Schuylkill County, or been indexed under a variant spelling. The negative result was expected to be non-contradicting and is consistent with the overall evidence.

---

### Conclusion

All five components of the Genealogical Proof Standard are satisfied. Research is declared exhaustive across all plausible repositories. Every factual claim carries a complete, accurate citation. All assertions have been classified by source type, information quality, and evidence type. The sole conflict (birthplace, c_001) has been resolved and does not affect the concluded relationship. The reasoning is laid out above in sufficient detail for independent evaluation.

The evidence **proves** that **Thomas Flynn is the father of Patrick Flynn** (b. ~1845, County Tipperary, Ireland; d. 12 March 1908, Schuylkill County, Pennsylvania). Two independent original sources with primary information — the 1845 Catholic baptismal register (recorded by the officiating priest, parents present) and the contemporaneous Flynn family Bible (recorded by the father himself) — directly establish the relationship. These are corroborated by two decennial censuses of co-residence (1850, 1860) and a 1908 death certificate naming Thomas as father. No source contradicts the parentage conclusion.

This conclusion represents the best answer that current evidence supports; it would be revised if previously unknown evidence required reassessment.

---

**Citations:**

[1] St. Mary's Roman Catholic Church (County Tipperary, Ireland), baptismal register, entry for Patricius Flynn, son of Thomas Flynn and Maria Brennan, baptized 12 March 1845; National Library of Ireland Catholic Parish Registers, microfilm 04173/04, image 287; digital image, NationalLibrary.ie, accessed 4 May 2026.

[2] Flynn family Bible (Holy Bible, Dublin imprint 1840), birth and family record pages, entry for Patrick Flynn, b. 1845, son of Thomas Flynn and Mary Brennan; original privately held by a Flynn descendant; digital images examined 4 May 2026.

[3] 1850 U.S. Census, Schuylkill County, Pennsylvania, population schedule, dwelling 84, family 91, Thomas Flynn household; NARA microfilm publication M432, roll 810; digital image, FamilySearch.org, accessed 1 May 2026.

[4] 1850 U.S. Census, Schuylkill County, Pennsylvania, population schedule, dwelling 84, Thomas Flynn household; digital image, Ancestry.com, accessed 1 May 2026.

[5] 1860 U.S. Census, Schuylkill County, Pennsylvania, population schedule, dwelling 112, family 119, Thomas Flynn household; NARA microfilm publication M653, roll 1141; digital image, FamilySearch.org, accessed 2 May 2026.

[6] Pennsylvania Department of Health, death certificate no. 4521 (1908), Patrick Flynn; Pennsylvania State Archives, Harrisburg; digital image, FamilySearch.org, accessed 3 May 2026.

[c_001] Conflict c_001: birthplace discrepancy between census/baptism sources (Ireland) and death certificate (Pennsylvania); resolved in favor of Ireland, per analysis above.

[8] FamilySearch probate index, Schuylkill County, Pennsylvania, 1870–1890; searched 4 May 2026; no Thomas Flynn probate record found (log_006).

fields.narrative_markdown:
## Parentage of Patrick Flynn (ca. 1845–1908)

The preponderance of available evidence strongly suggests that Patrick Flynn, born about 1845 in Ireland and died 12 March 1908 in Schuylkill County, Pennsylvania, is the son of Thomas Flynn of Schuylkill County, Pennsylvania. The mother's identity has not been established on current evidence.

### Evidence

**1. 1850 U.S. Census** — Patrick Flynn, age 5, born Ireland, appears in the household of Thomas Flynn, age approximately 32, born Ireland, in Schuylkill County, Pennsylvania (dwelling 84, family 91). *Source classification: original; information indeterminate* (the informant — most likely a household member, possibly a neighbor — cannot be identified from the record); *evidence indirect* (the 1850 census carried no relationship column; parentage is inferred from household position, shared surname, and age). This is the earliest documentary placement of Patrick with Thomas Flynn.

**2. 1860 U.S. Census** — Patrick Flynn, age 15, born Ireland, appears again in the household of Thomas Flynn in Schuylkill County (dwelling 112, family 119), with an age consistent with the 1850 enumeration. *Source classification: original; information indeterminate; evidence indirect* (the relationship column was not introduced until 1880; parentage is again inferred from household structure, shared surname, and consistent age progression). A full decade after the 1850 enumeration, Patrick remained in Thomas's household at an age when sons typically continued to reside with parents — a pattern consistent with the parent-child relationship.

**3. 1908 Death Certificate** — Pennsylvania death certificate no. 4521 for Patrick Flynn (died 12 March 1908, Schuylkill County) names "Thomas Flynn" as his father. *Source classification: original; information secondary* (the informant, James Brown, was Patrick's son-in-law — he is reporting what he knew about his father-in-law's parentage, not a fact he personally witnessed); *evidence direct* (the certificate explicitly names the father). This is the only direct statement of parentage in the current extracted evidence. The secondary nature of the informant's knowledge reduces this assertion's weight, but a son-in-law reporting the name of his father-in-law's father is unlikely to be wholly incorrect — he would have known Thomas Flynn by name.

### Corroborating Sources (Pending Full Extraction)

Four additional records bearing on this question have been located but await complete assertion extraction: (a) Thomas Flynn's 1881 will, Schuylkill County probate (src_006); (b) a deed from Thomas Flynn to Patrick Flynn, 1881, Schuylkill County Deed Books (src_008); (c) a St. Mary's Catholic Church, Pottsville, baptismal entry for Patrick Flynn, 1845 (src_005); and (d) a Patrick Flynn obituary in the *Pottsville Republican*, 1908 (src_009). The will and the deed are of particular significance: a testamentary transfer of land from Thomas to Patrick, or a deed that describes the parties' relationship, would substantially strengthen the parentage conclusion. Once assertions are formally extracted from these records, the conclusion should be reassessed and the tier reconsidered.

### Conflict Resolved

One conflict affects this conclusion (c_001): the 1850 and 1860 censuses record Patrick's birthplace as Ireland, while the 1908 death certificate states Pennsylvania. The two census records are contemporary original sources recorded within Patrick's early lifetime, with informants most likely present in the household. The death certificate was created 63 years after Patrick's birth by his son-in-law — a secondary informant with no firsthand knowledge of the birth event, who almost certainly confused Patrick's long-term place of residence with his place of birth. Under a GPS preponderance analysis, contemporary recordings by closer informants outweigh a later recollection by a secondary informant. Ireland is accepted as the birthplace; the Pennsylvania entry is attributed to informant error. This birthplace conflict does not affect the parentage conclusion.

### Assessment

The conclusion is rated **probable** rather than proved for four reasons: (a) both census sources provide only indirect evidence of parentage — they record household co-enumeration, not explicit parent-child declarations; (b) the death certificate's direct naming of Thomas as father comes from a secondary informant; (c) four corroborating sources (will, deed, baptism, obituary) are located but not yet fully extracted; and (d) research has not been declared exhaustive — the 1870, 1880, and 1900 censuses have not been searched. The mother's identity is not addressed by current evidence and remains an open question. If Thomas Flynn's 1881 will names Patrick as a son, or the baptismal register names Thomas as father, the conclusion would merit reassessment toward proved.

### Citations

1. 1850 U.S. Census, Schuylkill County, Pennsylvania, population schedule, dwelling 84, family 91, Thomas Flynn household; NARA microfilm publication M432, roll 810; digital image, FamilySearch.org, accessed 1 May 2026.
2. 1860 U.S. Census, Schuylkill County, Pennsylvania, population schedule, dwelling 112, family 119, Thomas Flynn household; NARA microfilm publication M653, roll 1141; digital image, FamilySearch.org, accessed 2 May 2026.
3. Pennsylvania Department of Health, death certificate no. 4521 (1908), Patrick Flynn; Pennsylvania State Archives, Harrisburg; digital image, FamilySearch.org, accessed 3 May 2026.

## Parentage of Patrick Flynn (ca. 1845–1908)

Patrick Flynn is **Probably** the son of Thomas Flynn of Schuylkill County, Pennsylvania.

### Evidence Summary

Three independent lines of evidence support this conclusion:

1. **1850 U.S. Census** (Original Source). Patrick Flynn, age 5, born Ireland, appears in the household of Thomas Flynn, age 32, born Ireland, in Schuylkill County, Pennsylvania (dwelling 84, family 91). The 1850 census does not state relationships, but Patrick's position in the household and shared surname are consistent with a parent-child relationship. The informant is indeterminate but likely a household member. This constitutes indirect evidence of parentage.

2. **1860 U.S. Census** (Original Source). Patrick Flynn, age 15, born Ireland, appears in the household of Thomas Flynn in Schuylkill County (dwelling 112, family 119). Like the 1850 census, the 1860 census does not state relationships explicitly (the relationship column was not introduced until 1880). Patrick's position in the household, shared surname, and age consistent with the 1850 enumeration support a parent-child relationship. This constitutes indirect evidence of parentage.

3. **1908 Death Certificate** (Original Source). Patrick Flynn's death certificate (no. 4521, Pennsylvania Department of Health) names "Thomas Flynn" as his father. The informant was James Brown, identified as Patrick's son-in-law. As a son-in-law reporting his father-in-law's parentage, Brown is a secondary informant for this fact — he was not a witness to Patrick's birth and is reporting what he was told. Nevertheless, this is direct evidence naming the father.

### Conflict Resolution

A birthplace conflict exists: the 1850 and 1860 censuses list Patrick's birthplace as Ireland, while the 1908 death certificate states Pennsylvania. The two census records are contemporary recordings with informants likely present in the household, while the death certificate was created 63 years after Patrick's birth by a son-in-law with no firsthand knowledge of the event. Per the GPS preponderance hierarchy, contemporary recordings by closer informants outweigh later recollections by secondary informants. Ireland is accepted as the birthplace; the death certificate entry is attributed to informant error.

### Assessment

The conclusion is rated **Probable** rather than **Proved** because: (a) both census sources (1850 and 1860) provide only indirect evidence of parentage (relationships not stated — the relationship column was not introduced until 1880), (b) the death certificate informant is secondary, and (c) research is not yet exhaustive — the 1870, 1880, and 1900 censuses have not been searched, and Thomas Flynn's probate records have not been located. If Thomas Flynn's will names Patrick as a son, or if additional census records confirm the relationship, the conclusion would advance to **Proved**.

### Citations

1. 1850 U.S. Census, Schuylkill County, Pennsylvania, population schedule, dwelling 84, family 91, Thomas Flynn household; NARA microfilm publication M432, roll 810; digital image, FamilySearch.org, accessed 1 May 2026.
2. 1860 U.S. Census, Schuylkill County, Pennsylvania, population schedule, dwelling 112, family 119, Thomas Flynn household; NARA microfilm publication M653, roll 1141; digital image, FamilySearch.org, accessed 2 May 2026.
3. Pennsylvania Department of Health, death certificate no. 4521 (1908), Patrick Flynn; Pennsylvania State Archives, Harrisburg; digital image, FamilySearch.org, accessed 3 May 2026.

entry.narrative_markdown:
## Who Were the Parents of Patrick Flynn (born ca. 1845, Schuylkill County, Pennsylvania)?

**Probable Proof Summary**

The preponderance of available evidence strongly suggests that Thomas Flynn of Schuylkill County, Pennsylvania, was the father of Patrick Flynn (born ca. 1845, Ireland; died 12 March 1908, Schuylkill County, Pennsylvania). No mother has been identified in the records searched to date. Three independent original sources — the 1850 and 1860 United States federal censuses and Patrick's 1908 Pennsylvania death certificate — consistently support this conclusion. Research is not yet declared exhaustive; probate records for Thomas Flynn have not been fully searched.

### Evidence

**1850 Federal Census (Original source; indeterminate information; indirect evidence)**

The 1850 U.S. Federal Census for Schuylkill County, Pennsylvania, enumerated Patrick Flynn, age 5, born Ireland, in the household headed by Thomas Flynn, age approximately 32, also born Ireland, at dwelling 84, family 91.¹ The 1850 census schedule did not include a relationship column; the father-son relationship is inferred from Patrick's position among household members, his age consistent with that of a young child of the head, and the shared surname. This constitutes indirect evidence of parentage. The informant for age and birthplace was most likely a household member — probably Thomas Flynn himself — but the census does not identify the informant, so information quality is classified as indeterminate. An independent Ancestry.com index of the same original record confirms the same household data.²

**1860 Federal Census (Original source; indeterminate information; indirect evidence)**

The 1860 U.S. Federal Census again placed Patrick Flynn, now age 15, born Ireland, in the Schuylkill County household of Thomas Flynn, at dwelling 112, family 119.³ The 1860 schedule likewise lacked a relationship column (that column was not introduced until 1880). Patrick's continued enumeration in Thomas Flynn's household a decade later, at an age consistent with the 1850 record, reinforces the parent-child inference. Information quality remains indeterminate for the same reasons as the 1850 record.

**1908 Pennsylvania Death Certificate (Original source; secondary information; direct evidence)**

Patrick Flynn's Pennsylvania death certificate, no. 4521, issued 14 March 1908, names "Thomas Flynn" as Patrick's father.⁴ This is direct evidence of the father-son relationship: the certificate explicitly records the parentage. The informant, however, was Patrick's son-in-law, James Brown, classified as secondary for birth and parentage facts — he was reporting information received secondhand, not from personal knowledge of Patrick's birth or parentage. Secondary informants on death certificates frequently introduce errors in birth and parentage data. Nevertheless, the certificate's consistent naming of Thomas Flynn as father corroborates both census enumerations.

### Correlation

Three independent original sources, created by different recorders across nearly six decades, converge on Thomas Flynn as Patrick's father. The two census records provide indirect evidence through household co-enumeration; the death certificate provides direct but secondary-quality evidence through explicit naming. No source names any other individual as Patrick's father, and no competing parentage candidate appears in the records examined.

### Conflict Acknowledged: Birthplace Discrepancy

The 1850 and 1860 censuses record Patrick's birthplace as Ireland; the 1908 death certificate records it as Pennsylvania. This conflict concerns a collateral fact (birthplace), not the concluded parentage relationship. It is resolved in favor of Ireland: the census records are original sources made closer to Patrick's birth, and their informants — most likely household members with personal knowledge — had greater proximity to the birth fact than the son-in-law informant on the death certificate, who may have confused Patrick's place of long residence with his birthplace, or may not have known Patrick was born in Ireland before immigrating as a young child. [c_001, resolved]

### Research Gaps and Tier Justification

Research on the parentage question has not been declared exhaustive. Probate and will records for Thomas Flynn in Schuylkill County (ca. 1870–1890) have not been fully searched; a will or administration record naming Patrick as a son would provide corroborating direct evidence. The 1870, 1880, and 1900 censuses for Patrick Flynn, as well as marriage and naturalization records, have not been searched within this project. Patrick's mother has not been identified.

**Tier: Probable.** Three independent original sources consistently support Thomas Flynn's paternity, and the only conflict (birthplace, c_001) is resolved. The tier does not reach *proved* because: (1) research is not declared exhaustive, and (2) the census records supply only indirect evidence of parentage (household co-enumeration, not explicit relationship statements). Conditional phrasing is applied accordingly.

---

*Sources:*

¹ 1850 U.S. Census, Schuylkill County, Pennsylvania, population schedule, dwelling 84, family 91, Thomas Flynn household; NARA microfilm publication M432, roll 810; digital image, FamilySearch.org (https://www.familysearch.org/ark:/61903/1:1:MXYZ), accessed 1 May 2026.

² 1850 U.S. Census, Schuylkill County, Pennsylvania, population schedule, dwelling 84, Thomas Flynn household; digital image, Ancestry.com, accessed 1 May 2026.

³ 1860 U.S. Census, Schuylkill County, Pennsylvania, population schedule, dwelling 112, family 119, Thomas Flynn household; NARA microfilm publication M653, roll 1141; digital image, FamilySearch.org (https://www.familysearch.org/ark:/61903/1:1:MABC), accessed 2 May 2026.

⁴ Pennsylvania Department of Health, death certificate no. 4521 (1908), Patrick Flynn; Pennsylvania State Archives, Harrisburg; digital image, FamilySearch.org (https://www.familysearch.org/ark:/61903/1:1:MDEF), accessed 3 May 2026.

warnings.advisory: Skill called MCP tools but the rubric has no dimension name suggesting tool-usage coverage (matched against keywords: ['tool usage', 'tool use', 'tool work', 'tool call', 'argument quality', 'argument-quality', 'response interpretation', 'tool selection', 'mcp tool', 'fixture']). The judge will grade other dimensions but won't score tool work explicitly. Consider adding a tool-usage dimension or renaming an existing one.

dimensions.name: Correctness
dimensions.rationale: The skill's outputs are factually correct. All three lines of evidence are accurately characterized: 1850 census as indirect evidence from co-residence with indeterminate informant, 1860 census as indirect evidence with same informant classification, and 1908 death certificate as direct evidence with secondary informant (son-in-law). The tier justification correctly contrasts probable with proved (exhaustive search not declared) and possible (three independent sources converge). The resolved conflict c_001 is properly explained in context. No fabricated material or unsupported claims appear in the narrative.

dimensions.name: Completeness
dimensions.rationale: The skill addressed all requirements: all three evidence sources are cited with evidence classification (indirect/direct, informant type), the resolved birthplace conflict c_001 is explicitly addressed with rationale for resolution, the tier is justified with explicit contrast to adjacent tiers, and the ParentChild relationship was written to the tree at probable tier. Nothing required by the input state was omitted.

dimensions.name: Tool Arguments
dimensions.rationale: All MCP tool calls passed validation with ok: true. The research_append call correctly appended ps_001 with the full supporting_assertion_ids array (a_004, a_005, a_008, a_009, a_010, a_013), resolved_conflict_ids [c_001], and narrative_markdown. The tree_edit call correctly added a ParentChild relationship (parent I2, child I1) sourced to S1, S2, S3 with quality 2. Both calls matched live fixtures with no fixture_not_found errors.

dimensions.name: Tier justification
dimensions.rationale: The tier (probable) is explicitly justified by contrasting with both adjacent tiers. The narrative explains why it does not reach proved: research is not declared exhaustive (pli_006 probate search still in progress) and census records supply only indirect evidence (household co-enumeration, no explicit relationship column). It explains why it is stronger than possible: three independent original sources converge on Thomas Flynn across six decades with no competing candidate. The distinction is clear and evidence-driven.

dimensions.name: Narrative standalone
dimensions.rationale: The narrative reads as a self-contained GPS conclusion. Every assertion referenced (1850 census facts, 1860 census facts, death certificate facts) is described in prose with inline superscript citations (¹²³⁴) and full source notes at the end. The conflict resolution (birthplace discrepancy favoring Ireland) is explained with reasoning. The tier is declared ('Tier: Probable') with conditional phrasing applied. A reader without access to research.json can follow the entire argument and understand why this tier was chosen.

dimensions.name: Evidence completeness
dimensions.rationale: Every relevant assertion and resolved conflict is cited in the narrative. The three evidence lines (a_004/a_005 from 1850 census, a_008/a_009/a_010 from 1860 census, a_013 from death certificate) are all incorporated with their evidence classifications explicitly stated. The resolved conflict c_001 (birthplace) is described in a dedicated section explaining its resolution. The narrative does not omit inconvenient evidence; it addresses the secondary-informant weakness of the death certificate alongside its corroborating strength.

dimensions.name: Proof-conclusion fit
dimensions.rationale: The chosen form (summary) correctly matches the evidence shape. Multiple correlated sources (three independent records spanning 1850–1908) that point clearly in one direction warrant a summary, not a statement or argument. No competing candidates exist; the only conflict (birthplace) is resolved outside the parentage conclusion. The narrative structure follows the summary form: opening assertion, evidence description (3 sources with classifications), correlation analysis, conflict acknowledgment, and tier justification. The form and structure align.

dimensions.name: Tree encoding
dimensions.rationale: At tier probable, the skill correctly wrote the concluded ParentChild relationship (Thomas Flynn I2 → Patrick Flynn I1) to tree.gedcomx.json via tree_edit operation add_relationship, with relationship ID R1. The relationship was sourced to all three sources (S1, S2, S3) with quality 2, as expected at probable tier. The tree now reflects the conclusion, not merely the narrative; the previously-unlinked persons are now linked. This satisfies the tree-encoding requirement for probable-tier conclusions.
